speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Survivors-- this is the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. The date is June 7, 1995. The interviewee is Joseph Roos. The interviewer is Merle Goldberg. The city is Los Angeles, the state California, the country the United States. And the interview will be conducted in English. My name is Merle, M-E-R-L-E, Goldberg. And I'm conducting an interview with Joseph Roos in Los Angeles, California, on Monday-- excuse me, Wednesday June 7, 1995. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Mr. Roos, we begin our interview by asking you several questions about yourself. First, if you could tell me your full name and spell it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely. My name is Joseph Roos. Joseph is easy to know how to spell. But Roos is very simple too-- R like in Robert, double O like in Otto, S like in Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, originally, my name was R-O-O-Z, like in Zebra. And when I arrived in the United States, I wrote a Z the way we learned it in Germany, without a sling on the bottom of it. But to the immigration authorities, it looked like an S.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the papers came back R-O-O-S. And when I became a citizen, I had my name changed legally R-O-O-S. And could you please tell me your birth date, the full birth--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on December 10, 1908.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, Austria. But I was a baby when the parents moved to Berlin in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Berlin where you were raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was raised and educated in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, unlike everyone else we interview, your story will be very different. And before we actually get into how you came to the United States, could you tell me a little bit, just briefly, about your family and about your parents in Germany-- what your father did, what your mother did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a merchant. He was not a very successful merchant. He was really born to be a singer. But his parents did not hold still for that. Go out and earn a livelihood. Later on, when we were four, and five, and seven years old, we used ask our father to sing for us. He had a beautiful voice. And it was a pity that he did not become a singer. He would have been a successful one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incidentally, he stopped singing when Hitler came to power. He never sang for us again until one specific incident. Do you mind me if I talk about that for a second? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was our parent's golden wedding anniversary in Chicago. My father, and my mother, and my two sisters, and my brother were helping create what was called Self Help. It was like an old people's institute intended primarily for people who were refugees, either from Nazi Germany or Nazi occupied countries. And there they had this beautiful anniversary celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this person spoke. And that person spoke. And then finally, somebody said, Mr. Roos, would you say something? And he says, when I was young, I used to enjoy singing. When Hitler came to power, I stopped singing. But in honor of my bride, I want to sing the song that I sang for her when I courted her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could hear this was a beautiful voice, but a broken old man's voice. People were just going like this-- crying. And I'll never forget that. I cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: What song did he sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Hungarian song. I wouldn't be able-- [NON-ENGLISH], which means I'm only a little child. The rest of it, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hungarian, but came to-- was sent to Vienna by his father to go to the School of Agriculture. Because the family owned vineyards. But when the father came to Vienna on some business, he went to the [NON-ENGLISH], where my father had a room. Oh, Mr. Roos is not here yet. He should be back from the conservatory any moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did-- did you say conservatory? He plunked himself down, relaxed for a little bit, and waited. That was the end. Go out and earn your own livelihood, not at my expense. But this one scene at the old people's home itself, it was a beautiful scene. Can you tell me a little bit about your brothers-- your brother and your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me just say one other thing, since you talked about family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's grandfather was one of the most famous rabbis in Hungary. He had his own Yeshiva, which is a place where you train people to become rabbis. He was the first one to be named after that famous rabbi, Maharam Shik. He died in 1880s-- in 1879. And my father was the first grandchild after him. So he was named after him-- Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you told me the other day an interesting story about when you've told people who your great, great grandfather-- about your-- about who your relative was. Do you want to tell me a little more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you've met some rabbis, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. I don't know exactly whether I understood you correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, on Monday when I was here at your house, you were telling me about this great grandfather from whom you're descended. And you were telling me that you met some people, and how honored they were to meet you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, learned people, who really know not only the history about the birth of the Israel or anything like that-- the learned people and who are Orthodox, if I say to them, I'm a descendant of the Maharam Shik, they say what is called a Shehecheyanu, which is a prayer, which thanks God for this moment that I had an opportunity to witness this, whether this particular thing or something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] one so-called rabbi was not a good human being. He was a dishonest man. And part of my job, about which I will speak later-- part of my job was to keep our skirts clean-- our meaning the skirts of the Jewish community, the individuals in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had word that this particular rabbi was in San Francisco and was what might be called a ganef. He was not an honest man. So I tricked him, in a way, to come to see me, where I was going to read him the Riot Act.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came. So the secretary says, Rabbi So-and-So is out there. I say, yeah, send him in. And he said two words. And I knew immediately, oh ho, a Hungarian accent. So I told him how I'm a descendant of the Maharam Shik. And he was about to say the Shehecheyanu, which I explained before what it is. I say, uh uh, don't say a Shehecheyanu, because by the time I'm finished with you, you will say a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is mentioned in the fifth book of Moses. It's a curse, where Moses says, if you're not going to live according to the way God wants you to live, he is going to punish you. [LAUGHS] So right away, he knew he was in a fight. I got him to behave as long as he was in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about your brother and your sisters a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was about four, almost five years younger than I. He-- all the rest of the family-- father, mother, one brother, and two sisters all remained in Germany when I left. They came from Germany into Czechoslovakia during the Hitler period. And then when Hitler took over Czechoslovakia, we succeeded in getting them out about a week or two weeks before the beginning of World War II. And it would take another two hours to describe to you how we succeeded in getting them out safe twice. He is a merchant. He is a successful one. He had the least of our education, and he is the one that is rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did explain something to me on Monday that I found very fascinating. How one of-- how-- was it your grandfather who had come to America? This-- that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That explains why you came to America to begin with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, originally, you know, there-- the intellectuals in Germany, Austria, Hungary made what they described as a revolution in 1847, '48. The leader of the revolution in Germany was a man by the name of Carl Shurz. S-C-H-U-R-Z. And when these intellectuals saw that you can't win a revolution without guns, those who could get out did get out before they would be captured and punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Shurz left. Shurz's chief pamphleteer and speechwriter was my grandfather on my mother's side. So when Shurz left, he left with him and came to the United States. His son, my grandfather, my mother's father, my grandfather married a Hungarian immigrant girl who was also a descendant of the same Maharam Shik-- see the relationship before they got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: That Hungarian wife-- Hungarian-born wife-- went to Hungary with her three children-- my mother among them, the oldest-- to meet the relatives, the mishpocha. On the way back, my grandmother died on the ship. So there are three children arriving in Chicago-- or maybe my grandfather met them in new York. That I don't know. Brought them to Chicago and didn't know what to do. Some people gave him advice. Go back and marry the sister. Blood is being thicker than water, you know, that line. So he married her sister, brought her with him and the children back to Chicago. And she was not happy in America. It wasn't kosher enough. No matter what, even if it says kosher outside of the butcher's shop, it's not kosher. And later on, I found out in my life that that is correct. Most of the so-called Jewish butchers are not kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she did not give him peace until he found a way to go back to Europe and earn a livelihood. So he went to Vienna. So that's where my mother grew up, in Vienna. And that's how it happened that I was born in Vienna. I was the last one to be born in Vienna. My kid brother was born in Berlin. And you also told me on Monday that your father wanted you all to-- your parents wanted you to come back to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, one thing-- my grandfather was very, very patriotic American in the finest sense, not the ugly sense of the phony patriots. On Friday nights, frequently when we were there for dinner, he would talk about America. I knew more about Jefferson as a kid than most of the kids of the same age in America. I knew more about Abraham Lincoln than many of the kids similar in age in America knew as much as I knew. When their sons grew up-- my uncle's-- there were four men and three women. Maybe I'm wrong-- four men. And as soon as my-- the oldest uncle was old enough to be shipped off to the United States, one after the other were sent off to the United States. Incidentally, they lived through World War I as enemy aliens. My father-- no, not my father-- my uncles and my grandfather had to report to the police once a week. You know, the police people knew them. But that was the law, so that they report to see enemy aliens are not going to do any damage. But as soon as the uncles were old enough, one after the other was shipped. And I was the oldest of the next generation. So it was a matter understood That wasn't even discussed. It was matter fully understood. When Joe is old enough-- to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came in 1927, when I was 18, after I finished my [NON-ENGLISH], which is the equivalent to two years of college here. What kind of schooling did you have growing up? Did you go to Cheder? Did you go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to a public school what is called a gymnasium. In Greece, the gymnasium wasn't only where you do the one, two, three, this, but also up here. So I finished gymnasium. And then two months after that, I left for the United States. On Sundays and on Wednesday afternoon, I went to Cheder. I did fairly well in school-- nothing to rave about. But I also had my first Nazi experiences there. In 1924, that would be-- I would have been 16 years old. 1924 incidentally was the year when Hitler was in jail writing

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was 16 years old. And this is when for the first time some of my classmates came to school with a leather arm band with a swastika. That's the first time. I was never invited to the home of any of my classmates, because I was different. I was a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one history teacher who enjoyed giving me the business. You know, in Germany then-- I don't know whether it's still there now. In Germany, you also went to school on Saturday. And my teacher-- history teacher-- would say, everybody pull out the books. We are going to have a test. And the kids would-- the boys-- we had no girls. They go, oh my God. They were not ready for it. But then after they had the books out, he would say, oh, I forgot-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh I forgot, Mr. Roos doesn't write today, because today is Shabbos. And the way he said Shabbos, he really gave it to me. And that's my experience even as a youngster. And maybe that had an influence on me later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it did have an influence--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: --can you describe-- so you're saying 1924 was your first experience. As the Putsches began, especially in Munich, do you remember? Can you describe for me what you might remember from that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the time, well, you know, fussball, what you call soccer football was the most famous sport-- as famous there as baseball was here in the United States. And in many of the areas in Berlin, which could be identified-- like here you could identify them by the zip code in your mail. Not far from where we lived, there was a little park-- the Teutoburg Park. Teutoburg was in the year nine, the Germans defeated the Romans in the Teutoburg Wald. Wald is world, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was our neighborhood. So in many of these neighborhoods, you had fussball clubs. And we had a Jewish fussball club. And we have to go and play against other teams. And we always won. And you know what drove us? Because people didn't like us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many a time, after the game was over, there was-- were fights, like you still have in Germany, not only by the children, by the old one. If the Italians and the Germans fight, many a time, they [INAUDIBLE], but not only the players themselves, the public too. So we had fights many a time. They'd call us the Schweinhunds, Judeschweinhunds-- you know, things like that. So that too had an impact on my life later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hitler was in jail. He got out. You left in '20-- 1927, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Um hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in those three years, can you describe for me anything you-- anything else that you might remember that might have even been going on, for instance, in Munich that you might have read about in the newspapers? I'm not sure, because you were in Berlin, that you would have witnessed anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the one thing that again had an impact on me later on was-- what do they call these people who blew up the big building in Oklahoma City-- they call them--

speaker: interviewer
text: The militia.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The militia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the militia. You have something similar to the militia there against the government. Because the government was run by the traitors who signed the Versailles Treaty. And the first thing they were going to do is eliminate that government and gain back what was called the Polish Corridor. Should I explain what the Polish Corridor is, or can I assume that they do know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think-- you can go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's one thing. The other thing is they were-- that this was the first time since the end of World War I that a Jew was a member of the cabinet-- Walter Rathenau-- Walter, W-A-L-T-E-R. You would say Walter-- Walter Rathenau. He was assassinated by those who are described as similar to the people that bombed the Federal Building in Oklahoma City. There's a same thing. You know, they wanted to get the Polish Corridor back. They wanted to have the right to have arms again, which was forbidden by the Versailles Treaty. And they were called traitors because they signed the treaty. They had to. And then there were other incidents. At least twice a month we-- first, I should say, food was a very, very little. There was very little food. We were four children, and we were allowed a half a pint of milk a day. I was going to say something else. I'm sorry. Give me one moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't remember. Can't remember. You were talking about the poverty. That's what you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the poverty-- how poor everyone was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah, I know. At least twice a month, we were told what a great victory ours-- our great heroes were winning here, and there, and everywhere, whether we were fighting against Russia, or Italy, or France. Twice a month, we had to hang out-- we didn't have to, but everybody hung out his flag-- great, great victory. Comes 1918, November 1918, and the-- our revolution began. And the soldiers quit. The laborers quit. They went on a general strike. The people were sold a bill of goods that everything was going well. We were shocked to discover that it wasn't so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of them discovered there were no victories. There were defeats. And some of them believed that they're victories. And there grew what was then-- what was-- what became known as the stab in the back. It was the socialists who stabbed our own troops into-- in the back. And that is why we were declared to have lost the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on this was built that which later on became the Hitler movement, you know. The traitors that are stabbed in the back and the fight against the Versailles Treaty.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were so young at that point in time, in 1918. What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I think? Well, I believe-- I don't know what I thought then. But I'm willing to bet that I accepted what others accepted. I mean, I was raised with the others to love my fatherland. So I would have believed some of these lies that were dished out by the Kaiser's government. Incidentally, I was in front of the Kaiser's palace when the revolution broke out. Interesting story-- I don't know whether I should bother you with it. The workers went on a general strike. The soldiers just left and came home-- those who found ways of getting home. Trains were not running. My father found a way of coming home. The main boulevard in Berlin is Unter den Linden. It's like here, Wilshire Boulevard. At the end-- no, at one end of Unter den Linden is that famous Brandenburg Tor. You've seen it again and again on television. And the-- I'll skip this. It's getting too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the other end of it was the Kaiser's palace. We lived about five minutes walk away from the Kaiser's palace. I heard shooting going on. So what does a young kid do? In 1918, I was 10 years old. I went over to where the noise was. So the revolution of people came there. And some people were shooting from the Kaiser's palace and from the Kaiser's church, which was across the street. They're shooting down at the people. Who were the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who came were the soldiers, who simply took over some of those big lorries, those big trucks and came to where the goings on were. You know, so one truck after the other. The trucks stops. People climb down and stand around. There was no organizing. There was nobody who said, now do this, and now do that. So there was standing around. And there was somebody who would be described by us in America as [GERMAN], maybe a low, low-ranking officer. And he was the only one who didn't tear off his medals or his insignia. And he shouted in German-- [GERMAN]. To step on the grass is forbidden. And you should have seen. Here the Germans were making a revolution. And here somebody in apparent authority says, verboten. You should have seen those Germans getting off of the grass. And this helps to understand the psyche of those Germans who went for a Nazi leader later on. It is verboten, he says. Even the mail carrier had the black pants and the red stripe. This is an officer. This is a government. You treat him nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is why-- this is the-- this story I've told again and again when I came to America. And there was a Hitler movement. And there was Hitler becoming chancellor. And people said, how come that the Germans-- the great writers, the great philosophers, the great singers, and the great musicians-- how did they hold still for what Hitler is doing to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them that story-- government-- you are afraid of government. You are afraid of government. You don't consider this. It's the government is for me. It's looking out for me. No. This is why a lot of people accepted a Hitler without a hesitation. Then were some. People would try to save people who were socialists, or social democrats, or even communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these experiences, you-- these experiences that you have just told me about, you took with you when you were a teenager and you already knew you were leaving for the United States. Is that how you always knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose it was meant-- things have many things. Above all is the fear of the unknown coming to a foreign country. People act differently, speak differently, and so on, and so forth. So it was one of many things. It was not the most important, the most outstanding impression.

speaker: interviewer
text: But since you asked that kind of question, that's why I put the stress on that instead of-- collecting stamps, does it mean anything to you? Sure, I collected stamps. Who doesn't, you know? What sort of a Jewish community did you grow up in? Religious? Assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Orthodox, which is to be expected because of what I told earlier. We belonged to what was called the Audacious Royal, which was the most impressive Orthodox congregation there. The men would go to the temple with striped pants, a Prince Albert coat. And some of them would come with a high hat, walking, you know, walking to the temple in a high hat. Others kept their high hats there, somewhere, you know, and changed their derbies, or whatever they wore before that before they went into the thing. But these were the Prussians. These were the Jews who happened to be Prussian or the Prussians who happened to be Jews. You could walk in at 10:30, and on a Saturday morning, you could tell before you walked in exactly at what point in the prayers they are. At one point, they would go upstairs to lift the Torah. I imagine everybody knows what the Torah-- to lift the Torah out and hand it to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typically Prussian, very Prussian-- and I said more than often than once, if Hitler had not been anti-Semitic, many of those Jews would have become Hitler supporters, because this whole fight against Versailles Treaty, which was a bad, bad thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Roos, we do have to stop, because we have to change the tape. I didn't want to interrupt you.
